result.markdown:
--- title: "The Case for R in HTA" subtitle: "Why transition from Excel and TreeAge?" author: "R for HTA (Basics) — Workshop 2026" institute: "RRC-HTA, AIIMS Bhopal | HTAIn, DHR" format: revealjs: theme: [default, ../../custom-theme.scss] slide-number: true footer: "R for HTA (Basics) — RRC-HTA, AIIMS Bhopal" logo: "" transition: fade width: 1280 height: 720 code-fold: true code-overflow: wrap highlight-style: github fig-align: center execute: echo: true eval: false warning: false message: false --- ## Why Are We Here? You have been doing HTA work — and doing it well — using Excel and TreeAge. . . . This session does not ask you to abandon what works. . . . It asks you to consider **what becomes possible** when you add R to your toolkit. ## The Limitations You Already Know PSA is painful - Complex macros or VBA required - Spreadsheet becomes slow and fragile . . . Reproducibility is difficult - Tracing nested cell references tedious . . . Version control is manual - Model_v3_final_FINAL_revised.xlsx? . . . Transparency for publication - Journals and agencies require open-source, auditable models ## What R Makes Possible **Reproducible** : Your entire analysis is a script — anyone can re-run it and get the same result . . . **Scalable** : 10,000 PSA iterations in seconds, not hours . . . **Transparent** : A reviewer can read your code and see exactly what you did . . . **Version-controlled** : Every change tracked, documented, reversible . . . **Free and extensible** : No license fees — thousands of packages for health economics ## The HTA Workflow in R ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(DiagrammeR) grViz(" digraph hta_workflow { graph [rankdir=LR, bgcolor='transparent', fontname='Helvetica'] node [shape=box, style='filled,rounded', fontname='Helvetica', fontsize=11] A [label='Define\nParameters', fillcolor='#4e79a7', fontcolor='white'] B [label='Build\nModel', fillcolor='#59a14f', fontcolor='white'] C [label='Run\nAnalysis', fillcolor='#f28e2b', fontcolor='white'] D [label='Sensitivity\nAnalysis', fillcolor='#e15759', fontcolor='white'] E [label='Visualise\nResults', fillcolor='#76b7b2', fontcolor='white'] F [label='Report &\nPublish', fillcolor='#edc948', fontcolor='black'] A -> B -> C -> D -> E -> F D -> B [style=dashed, label='Iterate', fontsize=9] } ") ``` ::: {#fig-hta-workflow .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` The complete modelling workflow ::: ::: ## Simple ICER: Excel Logic vs R **The Problem** New screening: ₹2,500 cost, 0.85 QALYs Standard care: ₹1,200 cost, 0.78 QALYs What is the ICER? . . . **In Excel** ✓ Works for one calculation ``` | Cell | Formula | |------|---------| | B6 | =B2-B3 | (Incremental cost) | B7 | =B4-B5 | (Incremental QALY) | B8 | =B6/B7 | (ICER) ``` . . . **Now what if you want 10,000 simulations?** ✗ Painful ## The R Code for ICER ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Define inputs cost_intervention <- 2500 cost_standard <- 1200 qaly_intervention <- 0.85 qaly_standard <- 0.78 # Calculate ICER incremental_cost <- cost_intervention - cost_standard incremental_qaly <- qaly_intervention - qaly_standard icer <- incremental_cost / incremental_qaly cat("ICER: ₹", round(icer, 0), "per QALY\n") ``` ::: So far, not obviously better than Excel... ## The Power: PSA in Three Lines ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Sample from distributions and calculate 10,000 ICERs icer_psa <- (rnorm(10000, 2500, 300) - rnorm(10000, 1200, 150)) / (rbeta(10000, 85, 15) - rbeta(10000, 78, 22)) cat("Mean ICER: ₹", round(mean(icer_psa), 0), "per QALY\n") cat("95% CI: ₹", round(quantile(icer_psa, 0.025), 0), "to ₹", round(quantile(icer_psa, 0.975), 0), "\n") ``` ::: **10,000 simulations in a fraction of a second.** Try doing that in Excel. ## The Cost-Effectiveness Plane ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggplot2) ce_data <- data.frame( inc_qaly = rbeta(10000, 85, 15) - rbeta(10000, 78, 22), inc_cost = rnorm(10000, 1300, 350) ) ggplot(ce_data, aes(x = inc_qaly, y = inc_cost)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.1, colour = "steelblue") + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") + geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") + labs(x = "Incremental QALYs", y = "Incremental Cost (₹)", title = "Cost-Effectiveness Plane: 10,000 PSA iterations") + theme_minimal() ``` ::: ## The Cost-Effectiveness Acceptability Curve ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} wtp_range <- seq(0, 300000, by = 5000) prob_ce <- sapply(wtp_range, function(wtp) { mean(inc_cost <= wtp * inc_qaly) }) ggplot(data.frame(WTP = wtp_range, Prob = prob_ce), aes(x = WTP, y = Prob)) + geom_line(colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 1.2) + geom_vline(xintercept = 100000, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") + annotate("text", x = 110000, y = 0.3, label = "WTP = ₹1,00,000") + labs(x = "Willingness-to-Pay (₹/QALY)", y = "Probability Cost-Effective", title = "Cost-Effectiveness Acceptability Curve") + theme_minimal() ``` ::: ## Beyond This Simple Example In this workshop, you will see R applied to **real HTA problems**: . . . - **Decision trees** with instant re-runs at different parameters - **Markov models** with probabilistic transition probabilities - **Partitioned survival models** with multiple extrapolation methods - **Shiny apps** for interactive policymaker tools ## Four Model Types We'll Cover ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} grViz(" digraph four_models { graph [rankdir=TB, bgcolor='transparent', fontname='Helvetica'] node [shape=box, style='filled,rounded', fontname='Helvetica', fontsize=11, width=2.5] center [label='HTA Models in R', shape=ellipse, fillcolor='#4e79a7', fontcolor='white', fontsize=13] dt_diag [label='Diagnostic\nDecision Tree\n(GDM)', fillcolor='#59a14f', fontcolor='white'] dt_ther [label='Therapeutic\nDecision Tree\n(DES vs BMS)', fillcolor='#f28e2b', fontcolor='white'] markov [label='Markov Cohort\nModel\n(CKD)', fillcolor='#e15759', fontcolor='white'] psm [label='Partitioned Survival\nModel\n(Breast Cancer)', fillcolor='#76b7b2', fontcolor='white'] center -> dt_diag center -> dt_ther center -> markov center -> psm } ") ``` ::: {#fig-four-models .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` HTA model types in this workshop ::: ::: ## What This Workshop Will NOT Do - Turn you into R programmers in three days - Ask you to memorise syntax - Expect you to write complex code from scratch ## What This Workshop WILL Do - Give you working templates you can adapt - Build confidence in reading and modifying R code - Show how to use AI tools to support your workflow - Help you understand when R is the right tool ## Key Takeaway R is not replacing your HTA expertise. . . . It is giving that expertise **a more powerful medium**. . . . The clinical judgement, model structure, parameter selection — that remains yours. . . . R simply makes the execution **more reproducible, transparent, and scalable**. . . . Let us get started.

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
